Kanner & Lorenzi [2026] FedCFamC1F 226 (12 March 2026)

Last Updated: 8 May 2026

FEDERAL CIRCUIT AND FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA

(DIVISION 1)

Kanner & Lorenzi [2026] FedCFamC1F 226

File number(s):
	
ADC 5046 of 2023

	

Judgment of:
	
STEWART J

	

Date of judgment:
	
12 March 2026

	

Catchwords:
	
FAMILY LAW – Parenting proceedings – Ex tempore Comments.

	

Division:
	
Division 1 First Instance

	

Number of paragraphs:
	
18

	

Date of hearing:
	
12 March 2026

	

Place:
	
Adelaide

	

Counsel for the Applicant:
	
Ms Rodriguez

	

Solicitor for the Applicant:
	
Daniel John Lawyers

	

Solicitor for the Respondent:
	
Mr Alevizos

	

Counsel for the Independent Children's Lawyer:
	
Ms Harris

	

Solicitor for the Independent Children's Lawyer:
	
Nicola Atchison

ORDERS

	
ADC 5046 of 2023

FEDERAL CIRCUIT AND FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA (DIVISION 1)

BETWEEN:
	
MR KANNER

Applicant

AND:
	
MS LORENZI

Respondent

	
INDEPENDENT CHILDREN'S LAWYER

ORDER MADE BY:
	
STEWART J

DATE OF ORDER:
	
12 MARCH 2026

THE COURT ORDERS THAT:

The CD/DVD produced this day by Suburb B South Australian Police be marked Exhibit “W-1”.
The USB stick created by the information technology officer of the FCFCOA this day be marked Exhibit “Court Aide Memoire-1”.
On or before 27 May 2026 the parties or any of them provide to the court a transcript of “W-1” and “Court Aide-Memoire-1” in English only, and or if the parties do not seek to rely on that exhibit they advise the chambers of Justice Stewart by email as soon as is reasonably practicable and always by 20 May 2026.
The matter is vacated and relisted to 13 July 2026 at 10am with ten (10) days allowed.
The matter is listed for case management hearing on 27 May 2026 at 9:30am.

AND THE COURT NOTES THAT:

The Court is making arrangements to ensure that the CD/DVD can be played on court equipment and to ascertain whether the contents can be uploaded and capable of being transferred to the parties via email.
Justice Stewart will not view the CD/DVD until further submissions are made on behalf of the parties save that she will be informed by the information technology officer of the court as to the length of the CD/DVD.
A copy of the court Aide Memoire-1 has been provided to the parties’ legal practitioners via email where the size of the files is able to be emailed, and the entire contents of the court Aide Memoire-1 provided on USB to the parties’ legal practitioners which is to be returned to the court before 4pm on 13 March
Upon joint written consent of all parties sent via email to Chambers, Justice Stewart review the CD/DVD or part thereof prior to 13 July 2026.
Justice Stewart will not review the CD/DVD unless the parties agree, otherwise it will need to be played in court in the usual way.
Section 114Q of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) provides that it is an offence punishable by imprisonment for up to one year to communicate to the public any account of family law proceedings which identifies the parties, witnesses or other people concerned with the proceedings, unless specifically authorised by the court.

Note: The form of the order is subject to the entry in the Court’s records.

Note: This copy of the Court’s Reasons for judgment may be subject to review to remedy minor typographical or grammatical errors (r 10.14(b) Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth)), or to record a variation to the order pursuant to r 10.13 Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).

Part XIVB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) makes it an offence, except in very limited circumstances, to publish an account of proceedings that identify persons, associated persons, or witnesses involved in family law proceedings.

IT IS NOTED that publication of this judgment by this Court under a pseudonym has been approved pursuant to subsection 114Q(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).

REASONS FOR EX TEMPORE JUDGMENT

STUART J:

These reasons were delivered Ex Tempore on 12 March 2026 and have been settled and corrected for grammatical errors and in order to convey the precise intention of the Court.

This matter comes before the Court in relation to two children, X, aged 6 years, and Y, aged 4 years. The matter was listed this week for seven days. Each of the parents, the father aged 52 years and the mother aged 40 years, are from Country C. English is their second language, and they require the assistance of interpreters.
A matter arose out of the family report, which was released on 22 May 2025 regarding some recordings which the mother allegedly provided to South Australia Police in December 2023. The family assessment report records as follows:
In South Australia Police information, it appears that the mother may have provided a USB with recordings, which appear to have been reviewed by the police and it was noted that, despite the language barrier, it was clear that the mother was coaching the child throughout the recording. The report writer did not have access to the USB footage.
The father in his trial material, which was filed on 17 February 20206, said as follows:
I am gravely concerned by the comments made by the family report writer at paragraph 26 of the family report, where it appeared apparent to the police that the mother was in fact coaching our child into making allegations of abuse against me. I seek that the mother disclose the USB recording that is referred to by the family report writer in this paragraph of this family report.

That appears to be first call for the USB recordings by the father. A second call for the USB recordings came via correspondence between the solicitors for the father to the solicitor for the mother last Friday.

An inquiry was made of the solicitor for the mother on the first day of trial. It seemed that the mother no longer held the recordings, and further inquiry revealed that the USB device was held by the Suburb B Police Station. Accordingly, orders were made that the mother obtain the recordings and that South Australia Police be requested assist her with that. Although it has taken some considerable time, today at approximately early afternoon, South Australia Police were able to provide the solicitor for the mother with a CD-DVD disc containing copies of the recordings. The police were unwilling to supply the USB device on the basis that that was to be held in police custody. Each of the parties have now, by various means, been provided with copies of those recordings through their various legal practitioners.
It is now 12 March 2026, and it is obvious that the case cannot proceed and conclude within the allocated time. Everybody agrees that that is so. There are a number of reasons why this case should not proceed part-heard, not the least of which is that things may become more complicated for the children if the case commenced but had not concluded. The case is complex and multi-layered. I note as follows: -
(a) The mother alleges that the father has sexually abused the children or either of them in numerous ways;

(b) The mother holds an unshakable belief by all reports that the children have been sexually abused;

(c) It is unclear whether the mother is capable of being disavowed of that view;

(d) The mother alleges physical abuse directly perpetrated upon the children by the father;

(e) The mother alleges that she has been physically abused by the father and has been the subject of family violence, including sexual abuse, by the father;

(f) According to Dr D, who has conducted a psychiatric assessment of the mother, she functions at a level described - I cannot recall the precise words, but she has low intellectual functioning;

(g) Dr D, who conducted a psychiatric assessment and psychosexual assessment with respect to the father, thought that he too was functioning at a low level intellectually, and recommended that the father undergo a neuropsychological report;

(h) Dr D assessed the father in a psychosexual sense as being low risk;

(i) The children have not seen the father for just over three years; and

(j) The children, according to the mother at least and with some verification in the family assessment report, do not appear to want to see the father with the older child X being noted to call the father "mean", although the family assessment report writer also noted, however, that there seemed to be little change in X’s demeanour while making that assessment.

Given the children's apparent attitudes towards the father, if the mother's evidence is accepted, there remain questions surrounding issues that if it is decided that the children should be reintroduced to the father (assuming that the Court makes a finding that there is no unacceptable risk of sexual abuse in the father's care), how that might be implemented and how it might impact on the children, or either of them, in a psychological and emotional sense.
There is a real issue that, assuming that there no unacceptable risk of sexual abuse applies, what findings should be made with respect to family violence. There then remains another issue as to, in the event that there is an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse, and in the event that family violence is not made out or it not made out to the extent that the father should be excised from the children's lives completely, whether the time, if it can proceed, would ever be capable of moving beyond supervised time, and even if was seen to be capable of moving on when that should occur.
In an overall sense, there is a real question as to the mother's capacity to promote a relationship between the children and the father, and if she is found to have limited or no capacity to do that, what the reasons might be for her presentation.
The parties require time to examine the recordings, and, in a realistic sense, even if I had decided to continue with the case, it likely would not have been able to start until Monday.
I do not want these reasons to be taken as an indication that I have made certain findings in the case. I have not. I will need to hear evidence. But, as I said earlier, the case is not without complexity, it is multi-layered, and if we peel back one layer, we are then confronted with it the next layer.
The children are represented by an Independent Children's Lawyer, whose preliminary view is that the father should be excised from the children's lives, that the mother should have sole parental responsibility, that there should be no time between the father and the mother, there be various injunctions pertaining to the father's ability to approach the children, and that there should be various injunctions in terms of his ability to contact the children even by card, letters and the like.
There is also a cultural overlay in this case, which needs to be considered, and that was also identified by the family assessment report writer as being difficult for a person who is not brought up in that cultural milieu to understand. That is, a factor which the Court will take into account in trying their best to assess the best interests of these children in an overall sense.
Accordingly, I have determined to adjourn the proceedings to 13 July 2026, which in relative terms is not too far down the track, although I accept that the parties, in particular the father, will feel that that is a long way away as he has been waiting three years for at least a chance to see his daughters.
The reason that I have decided to deliver these brief reasons is to make the point that this Court was ready, willing and able to start the case on Tuesday of this week being 10 March 2026, and it is purely by dint of the case being unprepared to proceed that it has been vacated. That is in the face of my assessment that seven days was going to be tight to hear a case with interpreters and people for whom English is a second language with allegations and counter-allegations of such moment and consequences of enormous impact on these children. Nevertheless, I have determined to adjourn the case. I have also listed the case for 10 days, which I think is a more realistic estimate for how long the case might take.
There was a request by counsel for the father that I stand the matter over to tomorrow so that they could utilise the benefit of a Court-funded interpreter. I have declined that request. The interpreters for each of the parties cost the Court in the vicinity of $2400 per day, which is an enormous impost on Federal Government funding of this Court in circumstances where the case will no longer proceed and that interpreters will need to be provided again for the hearing on 13 July 2026. The parties will need to source their own interpreters in order to examine these recordings and make assessments as to whether they seek to have them admitted, but I cannot see why that, for instance, could not take place via a telephone interpreter service. I was advised, I think, yesterday that the mother did not have an interpreter in her presence when she provided the USB device to the police, and the police were able to make an assessment at least on a preliminary basis of the content of that document.
Accordingly, that application was denied. These reasons will be transcribed and placed on the Court file so that anyone who comes to the file can understand what has occurred this week in a general sense, although I know that the matter will be re-listed before me, and it is unlikely that I will forget about the reasons for the adjournment between now and July 2026.
I will list the matter for a mention on 27 May 2026 for a case management hearing to ensure that the case is ready to proceed.
I will also make a further notation that if all parties agree, they can send a request into chambers that I view the recordings or part thereof prior to 13 July in order to save time; but I would not do that unless all parties agreed that that should occur, otherwise they will need to be played in court in the usual way. I am told, and the parties agreed that I could be told, that the recordings are about 30 minutes in length and are comprised of several different recordings.
